perturbation.original_text: (b) The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to divert or attempt to divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) employ or solicit, or initiate contact for employment with, any (A) director, officer or other senior or key employee of the Company or (B) any other employee of the Company whom the Receiving Party meets during its evaluation of the possible Transaction or about whom the Receiving Party receives Confidential Information; provided , however , a general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not 2 specifically targeting any such employees of the Company shall not be considered a solicitation or unauthorized hiring.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of six months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to divert or attempt to divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) employ or solicit, or initiate contact for employment with, any (A) director, officer or other senior or key employee of the Company or (B) any other employee of the Company whom the Receiving Party meets during its evaluation of the possible Transaction or about whom the Receiving Party receives Confidential Information; provided , however , a general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not 2 specifically targeting any such employees of the Company shall not be considered a solicitation or unauthorized hiring.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to six months may be insufficient to protect the company's interests and could be deemed unreasonable under New York law, where the agreement is governed. New York courts consider the reasonableness of such restrictions, and 6 months might be seen as too short, especially given the confidential information involved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law regarding reasonable non-compete and non-solicitation agreements.
perturbation.law_citation: Reed, Roberts Assoc., Inc. v. Strauman, 40 N.Y.2d 303, 307 (1976)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York courts require non-compete and non-solicitation agreements to be reasonable in time and scope. While there's no fixed rule, six months might be deemed unreasonably short, especially considering the employee's access to confidential information and the company's legitimate interest in protecting its customer relationships and workforce. The reasonableness depends on the specific facts, but a court might find this period inadequate, making the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-solicitation period from eighteen months to six months. The Law Explanation states that New York courts require non-compete and non-solicitation agreements to be reasonable in time and scope, and that six months might be deemed unreasonably short. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating that reducing the period could be deemed unreasonable under New York law. Although no law snippets were provided, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation clearly suggest that the reduced period may not meet the standard of reasonableness required by New York law, creating a legal contradiction as the modified period could be deemed unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Standstill Agreement . In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished to the Receiving Party pursuant to this Agreement, the Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of one year from the date of this Agreement (or, such shorter period agreed to by the Company with a third party who is provided access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction, the “ Standstill Period ”), unless expressly requested by the Company or its Board of Directors (or any committee thereof) in writing, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates not to and shall cause its and their respective Representatives acting at its and their respective behalf not to): (a) in any manner acting alone or in concert with others, acquire, agree to acquire or make any proposal to acquire, directly or indirectly, by means of purchase, merger, business combination or in any other manner, beneficial ownership of any securities of the Company, direct or indirect rights to acquire any securities of the Company (including any derivative securities with economic equivalents of ownership of any of such securities), any right to vote or to direct the voting of any securities of the
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Standstill Agreement . In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished to the Receiving Party pursuant to this Agreement, the Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of two years from the date of this Agreement (or, such shorter period agreed to by the Company with a third party who is provided access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction, the “ Standstill Period ”), unless expressly requested by the Company or its Board of Directors (or any committee thereof) in writing, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates not to and shall cause its and their respective Representatives acting at its and their respective behalf not to): (a) in any manner acting alone or in concert with others, acquire, agree to acquire or make any proposal to acquire, directly or indirectly, by means of purchase, merger, business combination or in any other manner, beneficial ownership of any securities of the Company, direct or indirect rights to acquire any securities of the Company (including any derivative securities with economic equivalents of ownership of any of such securities), any right to vote or to direct the voting of any securities of the
perturbation.explanation: Extending the standstill period to two years may be considered unreasonable under Delaware law, where the company is incorporated, potentially violating the directors' fiduciary duties. A two-year standstill is significantly longer than typical, and might unduly restrict potential bidders.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law regarding fiduciary duties of directors and reasonableness of defensive measures.
perturbation.law_citation: Unocal Corp. v. Mesa Petroleum Co., 493 A.2d 946 (Del. 1985)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Delaware law, as established in *Unocal v. Mesa Petroleum*, corporate directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of the company and its shareholders. Defensive measures, such as standstill agreements, must be reasonable in relation to the threat posed. A two-year standstill agreement is a significant restriction on potential acquirers and could be seen as disproportionate and therefore a breach of fiduciary duty, especially if it unduly restricts the market for corporate control.
perturbation.justification: The change extends the standstill period from one year to two years. The Perturbation Explanation argues that this extension may be unreasonable under Delaware law, potentially violating directors' fiduciary duties by unduly restricting potential bidders. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that defensive measures like standstill agreements must be reasonable in relation to the threat posed, citing *Unocal v. Mesa Petroleum*. A two-year standstill could be seen as disproportionate and a breach of fiduciary duty, especially if it unduly restricts the market for corporate control. Because the change introduces a significantly longer standstill period that might not be considered reasonable under Delaware law and potentially breaches fiduciary duty, a legal contradiction exists. Although no specific law snippets are provided, the legal analysis provided and the change of the contract language justifies a 'YES'.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Return of Confidential Information . Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term 4 hereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, (a) the Receiving Party’s legal department and/or outside counsel may keep one copy of the Confidential Information (in electronic or paper form) and, with respect to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who are accounting firms, such firms may keep one copy of the Confidential Information, in each case, if required to comply with applicable law or regulation and (b) the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain Confidential Information to the extent it is “backed-up” on its or their (as the case may be) electronic information management and communications systems or servers, is not available to an end user and cannot be expunged without considerable effort; provided, that any such information so retained pursuant to clauses (a) and (b) of this Section 7 shall be held in compliance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of eighteen months from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Return of Confidential Information . Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term 4 hereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, (a) the Receiving Party’s legal department and/or outside counsel may keep one copy of the Confidential Information (in electronic or paper form) and, with respect to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who are accounting firms, such firms may keep one copy of the Confidential Information, in each case, if required to comply with applicable law or regulation and (b) the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain Confidential Information to the extent it is “backed-up” on its or their (as the case may be) electronic information management and communications systems or servers, is not available to an end user and cannot be expunged without considerable effort; provided, that any such information so retained pursuant to clauses (a) and (b) of this Section 7 shall be held in compliance with the terms of this Agreement indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing indefinite retention of confidential information, even if backed up, conflicts with the principle of reasonable limitations on confidentiality obligations and potentially conflicts with data privacy regulations if the information contains personal data. New York law requires confidentiality agreements to be reasonable in duration.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law regarding reasonableness of confidentiality agreements and potentially data privacy laws if the information includes personal data.
perturbation.law_citation: Delta Filter Corp. v. Morin, 108 A.D.2d 991, 485 N.Y.S.2d 143 (3d Dep't 1985)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York courts generally require confidentiality agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration. While there's no absolute limit, indefinite confidentiality, especially coupled with the ability to retain copies indefinitely, could be deemed unreasonable, particularly if the information becomes outdated or publicly available through other means. This is because it creates a potentially perpetual restriction on the Receiving Party. Additionally, if the confidential information includes personal data of EU residents, this may trigger GDPR regulations if the company does business in the EU.
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the retention period of confidential information from eighteen months to indefinitely. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that New York law generally requires confidentiality agreements to be reasonable in duration, and indefinite confidentiality could be deemed unreasonable. Because the changed text now allows for indefinite retention, it contradicts the principle of reasonableness in duration for confidentiality agreements. The absence of scraped law snippets does not negate the explanations provided which directly point to the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 23. Termination . Except as otherwise specified herein, the obligations of the parties set forth in this Agreement shall terminate and be of no further force and effect eighteen months from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 23. Termination . Except as otherwise specified herein, the obligations of the parties set forth in this Agreement shall terminate and be of no further force and effect six months from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination period to six months may be insufficient to protect the disclosing party's confidential information. New York courts will consider the reasonableness of the duration of confidentiality agreements, and six months may be deemed too short given the nature of the information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law regarding reasonableness of duration of confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.law_citation: See Ashland Management Inc. v. Janien, 82 N.Y.2d 395 (1993)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law, as illustrated in *Ashland Management Inc. v. Janien*, requires that confidentiality agreements be reasonable in time and scope to be enforceable. A six-month duration may be deemed insufficient given the sensitivity of the disclosed information and the potential for long-term competitive harm if the information is misused. Thus, this change may contradict established legal principles concerning the duration of confidentiality obligations in New York.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Ashland Mgt. v. Janien :: 1993 :: New York Court of Appeals Decisions :: New York Case Law :: New York Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Ashland Mgt. v. Janien Annotate this Case 82 N.Y.2d 395 (1993) 624 N.E.2d 1007 604 N.Y.S.2d 912 Ashland Management Incorporated, Appellant, v. C. Christopher Janien, Respondent. (And a Third-Party Action.) Court of Appeals of the State of New York. Argued October 14, 1993. Decided November 22, 1993. Paul, Hastings, Janofsky & Walker, New York City (Lawrence I. Weinstein, Lisa M. Buckley and Roger M. Milgrim of counsel), for appellant. Davis & Gilbert, New York City (Michael C. Lasky, Bruce M. Ginsberg and Howard Weingrad of counsel), for respondent. Chief Judge KAYE and Judges TITONE, HANCOCK, JR., BELLACOSA, SMITH and LEVINE concur. *399SIMONS, J. This litigation involves a dispute between an investment management firm and a former employee. The employee developed a mathematical model for determining investment strategy and sought unsuccessfully to sell it to the firm. A dispute arose during the negotiations and plaintiff, Ashland Management Inc., instituted this action seeking to foreclose defendant from using his model. It alleged that the model is based upon trade secrets defendant misappropriated from the firm. Defendant claims he and plaintiff had agreed upon a contract for the use of the model and that plaintiff breached the contract. Defendant, therefore, counterclaimed seeking damages. After a nonjury trial, Supreme Court found that the parties had entered into a contract and that plaintiff had breached it. Accordingly, the court awarded defendant damages of $625,000 for lost profits. It held further that the information available to defendant did not constitute a trade secret of Ashland and therefore defendant was not guilty of misappropriation. The Appellate Division, after a minor modification, affirmed the judgment. The principal legal questions presented concern the availability of damages for lost profits under the rule stated in our two decisions in Ken
perturbation.justification: The change from eighteen months to six months for termination of obligations directly contradicts the principle established in New York law, particularly as referenced by *Ashland Management Inc. v. Janien*. The Law Explanation states that New York law requires confidentiality agreements to be reasonable in time and scope. The Perturbation Explanation asserts that six months may be insufficient to protect confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the *Ashland Mgt. v. Janien* case. The modified contract creates a legal contradiction because it sets a termination period that a court could deem unreasonably short, potentially rendering the confidentiality agreement unenforceable, directly undermining the established legal principle of 'reasonableness' in confidentiality agreements.

perturbation.original_text: 8. 2012 Annual Stockholders Meeting . The Company agrees (a) that the meeting date for its 2012 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 28, 2012) will be held no earlier than thirty-one days after the first anniversary of the Company’s 2011 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 29, 2011) and (b) to provide the Receiving Party a copy of the press release announcing the date of its 2012 annual meeting on the date such press release is publicly issued.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. 2012 Annual Stockholders Meeting . The Company agrees (a) that the meeting date for its 2012 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 28, 2012) will be held no earlier than three hundred and sixty-five days after the first anniversary of the Company’s 2011 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 29, 2011) and (b) to provide the Receiving Party a copy of the press release announcing the date of its 2012 annual meeting on the date such press release is publicly issued.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the 2012 annual stockholder meeting by 365 days would violate Delaware law requiring annual meetings. Delaware law mandates that companies hold an annual meeting for the election of directors.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law requiring annual meetings.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware General Corporation Law Section 211(b) mandates that a corporation hold an annual meeting for the election of directors. Deferring the meeting by 365 days from the anniversary of the previous year's meeting is a clear violation of this legal requirement, as it essentially skips an entire annual meeting cycle. Shareholders have a right to elect directors yearly, and this change would deprive them of that right.

General Corporation Law Subchapter VII. Meetings, Elections, Voting and Notice Â§ 211. Meetings of stockholders. (a) (1) Meetings of stockholders may be held at such place, either within or without this State as may be designated by or in the manner provided in the certificate of incorporation or bylaws, or if not so designated, as determined by the board of directors. If, pursuant to this paragraph or the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws of the corporation, the board of directors is authorized to determine the place of a meeting of stockholders, the board of directors may, in its sole discretion, determine that the meeting shall not be held at any place, but may instead be held solely by means of remote communication as authorized by paragraph (a)(2) of this section. (2) If authorized by the board of directors in its sole discretion, and subject to such guidelines and procedures as the board of directors may adopt, stockholders and proxyholders not physically present at a meeting of stockholders may, by means of remote communication: a. Participate in a meeting of stockholders; and b. Be deemed present in person and vote at a meeting of stockholders, whether such meeting is to be held at a designated place or solely by means of remote communication, provided that (i) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to verify that each person deemed present and permitted to vote at the meeting by means of remote communication is a stockholder or proxyholder, (ii) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to provide such stockholders and proxyholders a reasonable opportunity to participate in the meeting and to vote on matters submitted to the stockholders, including an opportunity to read or hear the proceedings of the meeting substantially concurrently with s

perturbation.justification: The changed text delays the annual stockholder meeting by 365 days, effectively skipping a year, whereas Delaware law requires annual meetings for the election of directors. This directly contradicts the core requirement of holding an annual meeting. The scraped snippets, specifically 8 DE Code § 211 (2023), address 'Meetings of stockholders,' implying a regular, recurring obligation, which the modification undermines. The modification introduces a clear violation by not adhering to the implicit requirement of holding annual meetings.
